prompt: Which work in the corpus describes a real incident in which slave-owners, organized as a 'secret committee' calling themselves 'respectable gentlemen', issued a proclamation summoning a Sunday mob with 'the necessary tools' to demolish a Methodist chapel, then over two days tore down the building, ripped up Bibles, made flags from the missionary's linen, gave 'three times three' ferocious howls of victory, and afterward published a triumphant proclamation dated from Bridgetown on 21 October 1823? Quote the dated proclamation announcing that 'at midnight they completed the ruin of the church', and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The 21 October 1823 Bridgetown (Barbados) Methodist chapel demolition, with its 'three times three' howls, the linen-flags detail, the 'respectable gentlemen' self-designation and the verbatim proclamation declaring the missionary fled to St. Vincent, is a uniquely specific journalistic episode that appears nowhere else in the corpus. Vols I–III of Comte never reach this Caribbean material; the Spanish-American volumes (Comte's property treatises and Dunoyer) treat different matter; no other corpus work concerns West Indian abolitionism.

prompt: Which work in the corpus enumerates the five kinds of papal indulgence-bulls that the Roman clergy delivered to the Spanish government for resale in its American colonies — naming them as 'the bull for the living, that for the dead, that for dairy and eggs, that of composition, and that of the crusade' — and explains that the bull of composition could turn an unjust holder of stolen goods into the 'legitimate owner' provided he handed the indulgence-merchant a small share of his prize? Quote the five-bull enumeration and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The exact five-fold enumeration (vivants / morts / laitage et œufs / composition / croisade) and the description of the 'composition' bull as a money-back laundering of stolen property are uniquely Vol IV. No other corpus work treats Spanish-American indulgences in this taxonomic detail; Comte's other volumes do not discuss the papal-bull traffic; the Dunoyer, Iqbal, Cynewulf, and Sanskrit works are wholly off-topic.

prompt: Which work in the corpus, while arguing that emancipated labor is more productive than coerced labor, claims that 'ten English workers do more work, in a given time, than fifty Russian slaves' and that an English lord who possesses the same extent of land as a Russian lord 'is ten times richer than he, although he does not possess a single slave, while the second possesses thousands'? Quote the comparison and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The very specific 10-English-workers-versus-50-Russian-slaves ratio, paired with the ten-times-richer English lord vs. thousand-slave-owning Russian lord, is a distinctive abolition-economics factoid. It does not appear in Vols I–III, which treat (respectively) civilization/savagery method, climate/race, and US slavery; no other corpus work makes this England-Russia comparison.
